pattern: The frog's state is its current stone plus the size of the jump that brought it there, so memoizing reachable (stone, jump) pairs and branching to jumps of k-1, k, and k+1 explores every option without revisiting a state.
hints: The only thing that matters at a stone is which jump sizes can land you there, because the next jump must be one of k-1, k, or k+1.
Track, for each stone, the set of jump sizes that can reach it; the first jump from stone zero is fixed at one unit.
Propagate forward: from each reachable (stone, k) try landing on the stone at position stone + (k-1), stone + k, stone + (k+1); success is reaching the last stone.

edge_inputs.description: a gap too wide to clear
two adjacent stones, one unit apart, the minimal crossable river
two stones more than one unit apart cannot be crossed since the first jump must be one
jumps must grow then can hold or shrink
a stone exactly two units after the start is unreachable on the mandatory first jump

stress.description: build a guaranteed-crossable river by taking a valid jump sequence, then sprinkle extra reachable stones
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(60, size))
    pos = 0
    k = 1
    stones = [0]
    for _ in range(n - 1):
        choices = [j for j in (k - 1, k, k + 1) if j >= 1]
        k = rng.choice(choices)
        pos += k
        stones.append(pos)
    extra = sorted(set(rng.randint(1, pos) for _ in range(n // 3)))
    stones = sorted(set(stones) | set(extra))
    return [stones]
stress.note: embedding a real valid jump path keeps these instances crossable while extra stones add decoys

stress.description: random strictly increasing positions that are usually not crossable
stress.note: random spacing typically produces uncrossable rivers, exercising the false path
